briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. We will now begin the climate briefing for March 23, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis and Call for Urgent Action
sections.content: A study has revealed that if global temperatures rise by 2 degrees Celsius, the number of countries facing severe food insecurity will nearly triple, with the deterioration of food systems in low-income countries expected to be particularly rapid [61]. Furthermore, the period from 2015 to 2025 has been identified as the warmest decade on record, once again emphasizing the severity of the climate crisis [52]. A report indicates that Australia's Generation Alpha will face an additional lifetime cost burden of $185,000 if no urgent action is taken on the climate crisis, warning of the economic impact on future generations [36, 60], and the necessity for urgent action is being stressed, alongside warnings about the impact of climate change on extreme weather [37, 55].

sections.title: ⚠️ Challenges in Climate Action and Concerns over Greenwashing
sections.content: As a 'speed war' to achieve carbon neutrality begins, discussions on the timing of greenhouse gas reductions are active [12], and concerns have been raised that the implementation of K-RE100 by public institutions may not lead to actual carbon reductions and could instead be 'greenwashing' [33]. Internationally, the possibility that China's renewable energy industry is entering a 'waste era' has been raised, making the recycling of solar and wind power facilities an important challenge [26], and policy frictions are also occurring, such as Wisconsin suing the EPA over the cessation of climate change response measures [44]. Furthermore, an analysis suggesting that CO2 emissions from new data centers in the UK could be 'hundreds of times' higher than expected indicates the need to re-evaluate the environmental impact of digital infrastructure [62].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic Carbon Neutrality Practices and Climate Change Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts are underway to achieve carbon neutrality and adapt to climate change. KIOST and Pusan National University have signed a business agreement to respond to the climate change crisis, strengthening cooperation in marine science and technology [3, 7], and KT&G has been selected as an excellent company in climate change and water resource management, demonstrating its responsible corporate role [4, 5]. In local communities, various local governments, including the start of Gumi's 'Carbon Neutral Industrial Complex,' Naju City, Chungnam Province, Gangdong-gu, Cheongju City, and Cheonan City Council, are undertaking specific practices such as tree planting, waste reduction, establishing leading models for carbon neutrality in public buildings, and raising greenhouse gas reduction targets [8, 9, 10, 11, 13, 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 22, 24]. Additionally, organizations like Incheon Gyeyang-gu Saemaul Association, Samjeong Elementary School, and Gwangmyeong City are encouraging carbon neutrality practices through environmental cleanup and sapling planting campaigns [20, 21, 23], and institutional and cultural efforts for climate change adaptation are also being pursued, including considering climate change in rice yield predictions [1], research on post-management measures for climate change impact assessments in urban development projects [2], and seeking digital transformation and climate crisis solutions in cultural heritage preservation [6].

sections.title: 🇰🇷🌍 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Innovation Trends
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is steadily accelerating both domestically and internationally. Taedam, a specialized company in renewable energy solar power generation facilities, was recognized for its technology by winning the '2026 Korea NO.1 Industrial Award' [25], and XCF Global is pursuing a strategy to strengthen renewable energy security by producing 38 million gallons of Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) annually [29]. Gunsan City is promoting the designation of an offshore wind power complex, officially launching region-win-win renewable energy projects [32], and Hana Bank demonstrated the financial sector's interest by conducting on-site training visits for new and renewable energy [34]. Meanwhile, groundbreaking research is discovering natural ways to combat climate change [35], and creative approaches to enhance understanding of climate change are also continuing, such as a University of Connecticut researcher converting sea level data into music [45].

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Awareness and Response to Climate Change
sections.content: In the international community, awareness of climate change is deepening, and responses are being made in various ways. There was a climate litigation update from the Sabin Center for Climate Change Law [43], and legal and ethical discussions are active, with attempts by oil magnates to evade responsibility for climate change damages being observed [46]. Regarding the New York Governor's climate change policy proposals, environmentalists expressed anger, while business groups welcomed them, showing conflicting reactions that suggest difficulties in policy implementation [47], and an artist from Saugatuck unveiled an exhibition on the theme of climate change, attempting to convey messages through art [50]. Furthermore, the possibility that climate change could complicate avalanche risks in the Pacific Northwest has been raised [39], and scientific observations and warnings continue, with research showing that wetland butterflies are proving to be a new 'canary' for climate change [51].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted warnings about the climate crisis, and we confirmed that diverse efforts are continuously being made both domestically and internationally. Amidst the complex challenges of climate change, we must build a sustainable future through cooperation and innovation. This concludes today's climate briefing.

summary.phenomenon: According to new Deloitte modeling, if Australia does not take urgent action on the climate crisis, the country's Alpha generation is expected to bear a lifetime cost of $185,000. Millennials are projected to face $130,000, and Gen Z $165,000.

articles.original_title: Far more countries face critical food insecurity if world heats up by 2C, analysis shows
articles.description: Exclusive: Food systems of low-income nations projected to deteriorate seven times as fast as those of wealthy onesThe number of countries falling into critical food insecurity could almost triple to 24 if global temperatures increase by 2C, research has shown.Analysis by the International Institute for Environment and Development (IIED) shows the climate crisis will disproportionately affect food systems in poorer nations, widening the gap between the most and least vulnerable countries. Contin

summary.phenomenon: Research indicates that if global temperatures rise by 2 degrees Celsius, the number of countries facing critical food insecurity could almost triple to 24. Food systems in low-income nations, in particular, are projected to deteriorate seven times faster than those in wealthier countries.
summary.cause: This is due to the climate crisis disproportionately affecting food systems in poorer nations.
summary.outlook: This is expected to further widen the gap between the most and least vulnerable countries.

articles.original_description: Exclusive: Food systems of low-income nations projected to deteriorate seven times as fast as those of wealthy onesThe number of countries falling into critical food insecurity could almost triple to 24 if global temperatures increase by 2C, research has shown.Analysis by the International Institute for Environment and Development (IIED) shows the climate crisis will disproportionately affect food systems in poorer nations, widening the gap between the most and least vulnerable countries. Contin

articles.description: Emissions from the new data centres set to drive the UK’s AI “revolution” could be... The post Analysis: CO2 from UK data centres could be ‘hundreds of times’ higher than thought appeared first on Carbon Brief.

summary.phenomenon: An analysis suggests that carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions from the UK's new data centers could be 'hundreds of times' higher than previously estimated.
